nativeRange.habitat: No known natural populations. The species is widely believed to have arisen in the German aquarium trade in the mid-1990s, likely from a single specimen of the closely related Procambarus fallax (native to Florida and southern Georgia). Established invasive populations now occur in slow-moving lentic freshwater habitats — marshes, ditches, ponds, drainage channels, and quarry lakes — across more than twenty countries on five continents.

decorPreferences: PVC pipes and tubes with multiple entrances
rock caves and stacked stone shelters
sand or fine gravel substrate suitable for burrowing
no live plants (will be uprooted, shredded, and eaten)

diet.notes: Generally considered an opportunistic omnivore and detritivore that has been reported to eat almost anything offered. The bulk of the diet is typically detritus, biofilm, soft algae, decaying plant matter, and sinking pellets, supplemented one or two times per week with protein items such as blackworms, earthworms, brine shrimp, snails, or small frozen foods. Calcium-rich supplements (cuttlebone, blanched kale, eggshell) are commonly recommended to support successful molting.

goodWith: fast mid- and top-water dither fish at the keeper's risk only
avoidWith: slow or bottom-dwelling fish (corydoras, loaches, plecos)
dwarf shrimp and other small invertebrates
other crayfish and lobsters (territorial, will fight)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Chucholl C.; 3030x1960. Vibrant olive-green individual with the classic marbled carapace pattern and bluish spotted claws, sharp and whole in frame among aquatic vegetation - best diagnostic view of the species.
Photographer Andre Karwath (Aka); 3513x2418. Crisp full side profile of a tan/brown marbled morph on rock - shows the species' color variation and marbled markings clearly.
Photographer Lorenz Seebauer; 6000x4000. Sharp in-situ shot of a reddish-brown individual on sand, whole animal in frame - illustrates the species' color variability.

summary: An all-female freshwater crayfish that reproduces by cloning — every individual is genetically identical. Generally regarded as undemanding to keep, but banned across the EU and in several US states and Canadian provinces due to extreme invasive risk.
careNotes: LEGAL / RESPONSIBLE-OWNERSHIP CAVEAT (read first): The Marbled Crayfish is among the most heavily regulated species in the aquarium hobby because of its extreme invasive potential. The species has been listed since 2016 as an Invasive Alien Species of EU Concern (Regulation 1143/2014), making it illegal across the entire European Union to possess, keep, breed, transport, sell, or release. Japan banned the species in 2020. In the United States it is prohibited or restricted at the state level in (at minimum) Michigan, Idaho, Missouri, Tennessee, and Maryland, with additional restrictions reported in Arizona, California, Georgia, Hawaii, Minnesota, Nevada, Ohio, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Utah, and Wisconsin; the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has also proposed adding it to the federal Lacey Act injurious-wildlife list. In Canada it is banned in Ontario and Saskatchewan. Anyone considering this species must verify the current legal status in their own jurisdiction before acquiring one, and under no circumstances should specimens, juveniles, eggs, or even tank water containing eggs be released to natural waterways — a single individual can found a self-sustaining clonal population. Husbandry-wise the species is generally regarded as hardy and undemanding; it tolerates a wide range of temperatures (reportedly surviving brief excursions below 6°C and above 30°C), pH 6.0–9.0, and most aquarium hardness values, with optimal activity and reproduction in the 18–25°C / pH 7.0–8.0 / gH 3–18 range cited above. A minimum tank of 100 L (around 75 cm long) is advisable rather than the smaller 20-gallon floor seen in some retail care guides: adult females reach 7–13 cm, are highly destructive to plants and substrate, and produce very large broods that quickly overwhelm smaller setups. A tightly-fitting lid is essential — escape behaviour is consistently described as a defining trait. The species is best kept as a single specimen in a species-only tank; mixing with fish, shrimp, snails, plants, or other crayfish is broadly cautioned against.
breedingNotes: Reproduction is obligately parthenogenetic — the species consists entirely of triploid females (carrying 276 chromosomes) that produce genetically identical clonal offspring without any need for males. As a result, breeding is effectively inevitable: any sexually mature individual kept above roughly 16°C will eventually carry eggs. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 5–7 months, at around 3.5 cm body length. Clutches commonly contain 45–700 eggs, with averages of around 200–420 surviving juveniles per cycle. Incubation takes approximately 22–42 days, and the interval between clutches runs roughly 50–85 days — an annual output that can exceed 1,000 offspring per female. The cloning biology, large brood sizes, and broad environmental tolerance are exactly what make the species such a severe invasive risk, so keepers must plan ahead for what to do with offspring. They cannot legally or ethically be released, and rehoming options are very limited in jurisdictions where the species is banned.

primary.notes: Designated primary source did not surface a dedicated Marbled Crayfish care guide on the homepage and on-site search was not accessible via WebFetch. Primary husbandry data was sourced from Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder) instead; URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated secondary source returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on both the homepage and the known marbled-crayfish news article (https://www.practicalfishkeeping.co.uk/fishkeeping-news/illegal-marbled-crayfish-poses-threat-to-uk/). Substituted with Aquarium Breeder, USGS, USFWS, and Wikipedia. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
additional.notes: Substitute primary. Source for adult size 7-12 cm, lifespan 2-3 years, water parameters (18-25°C optimum, pH 7-8, gH 3-18, kH 3-15), ~20-gallon minimum tank, omnivorous diet, semi-aggressive temperament, high escape risk, parthenogenetic breeding biology (45-700 eggs/clutch, 22-42 day incubation, ~5-7 month sexual maturity), and EU/state ban summary.

additional.name: USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species — Marbled Crayfish
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~13 cm), temperature optimum 18-25°C with extreme tolerance 8-30°C+, lentic / wet-prairie / marsh habitat preference, obligate-parthenogenetic biology, and US legal status (banned in Missouri; potential Lacey Act injurious listing).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Marbled Crayfish
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback (family Cambaridae, order Decapoda, class Malacostraca; synonym Procambarus fallax forma virginalis; described as P. virginalis by Lyko 2017), confirmation of 1995 German aquarium-trade origin, triploid 276-chromosome biology, and legal status (EU ban 2016, Japan ban 2020, US state bans including Idaho/Michigan/Missouri/Tennessee/Maryland, Canadian bans in Ontario and Saskatchewan).

additional.name: U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service — Ecological Risk Screening Summary
additional.notes: Federal regulatory reference for the proposed Lacey Act injurious-wildlife listing and ecological-risk profile (cited via search summary; PDF not fetched directly).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Adults are reported to molt roughly two to three times per year; juveniles molt much more frequently as they grow. Molt intervals shorten at higher temperatures and lengthen in cooler water. Temporary post-molt colour loss (lasting around 7–10 days) and short-term hiding are normal, and the shed exoskeleton is commonly re-consumed for calcium and should not be removed. Like other crustaceans the species is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and snail treatments, which should be avoided entirely.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Obligately parthenogenetic — reproduction is essentially inevitable for any sexually mature individual kept under normal aquarium conditions. See top-level breedingNotes for biology and offspring-management cautions.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Banned across the entire European Union (Invasive Alien Species of Union Concern, 2016), in Japan, and in multiple US states (including Michigan, Idaho, Missouri, Tennessee, Maryland) and Canadian provinces (Ontario, Saskatchewan). Verify your local legal status before acquisition, and never release this species — even tank water containing eggs — to natural waters.
